revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Hasinaw-uk-kamuy]][[en:Hasinaw-uk-kamuy]][[ja:ハシナウウクカムイ]]
{{Short description|Ainu goddess}} '''Hasinaw-uk-kamuy''' (ハシナウ・ウｸ・カムイ; also '''Hash-Inau-uk Kamuy''', '''Hashinau-uk Kamuy''' or simply '''Hash-uk Kamuy''') is the {{ill|Ainu people|en|Ainu people|qid=Q101828|lt=Ainu|1=Ainu people}} ''[[kamuy]]'' (''goddess'') of the hunt. She is sometimes called '''Isosange Mat''' (''Bringing-down-game Woman'') and '''Kamuy Paseguru''' (''Potent Kamuy'').
==Depiction== Hasinaw-uk-kamuy is depicted as a woman with long hair who wields a bow and arrows, who often carries a child on her back. She is accompanied by, or sometimes appears in the form of, a small bird, which shows hunters the way to game. She is also represented by the {{ill|Aconitum|en|Aconitum|qid=Q155904|lt=aconite|1=Aconitum}} plant, with which Ainu hunters poisoned their arrows.<ref name="ash">Ashkenazy, Michael. ''Handbook of Japanese Mythology''. Santa Barbara, California: ABC-Clio, 2003. 161</ref>
==Mythology== Hasinaw-uk-kamuy is a deity of great importance to the Ainu, who historically subsisted largely on hunting, fishing, and gathering. She was born from the fire-producing drill, and is sometimes said to be the sister of [[Kamuy-huci]], the hearth goddess, or of {{ill|Shiramba Kamuy|en|Shiramba Kamuy|qid=Q7498251|lt=Shiramba Kamuy|1=Shiramba Kamuy}}, god of vegetation. Hunters worshipped her to assure a successful hunt, and fishermen to ensure a full catch. She is also invoked to aid in childbearing.<ref name="ash"/>
==Notes== <references/>
==References== *Ashkenazy, Michael. ''Handbook of Japanese Mythology''. Santa Barbara, California: ABC-Clio, 2003. *Etter, Carl. ''Ainu Folklore: Traditions and Culture of the Vanishing Aborigines of Japan''. Chicago: Wilcox and Follett, 1949. *Munro, Neil Gordon. ''Ainu Creed and Cult''. New York: Columbia University Press, 1995.
{{Japan-myth-stub}} {{Jmyth navbox long}}
